para.text: Area. All of the waters upstream of a line beginning at a point at or near Lat. 38° 39.680' N., Long. 75° 47.815' W., then running 147° True to a point at or near Lat. 38° 39.653' N., Long. 75° 47.793' W.
The speed limit for the area described in §A of this regulation is 6 knots all year.

para.text: The Patuxent River south shore areas, with their corresponding speed limits, are set forth in §§B—F of this regulation.
Cuckhold Creek.
Areas.
All of the waters upstream of a line beginning at a point at or near Lat. 38° 20.988 N., Long. 76° 30.857 W., then running 344° True to a point at or near Lat. 38° 21.257 N., Long. 76° 30.953 W.

All of the waters enclosed by a line beginning at a point at or near Lat. 38°20.345' N., Long. 76°30.118' W., then running 294° True to a point at or near Lat. 38°20.392' N., Long. 76°30.252' W., then running along the shore to a point at or near Lat. 38°20.580' N., Long. 76°30.200' W., then running 29° True to a point at or near Lat. 38°20.688' N., Long. 76°30.125' W., then running along the shore to a point at or near Lat. 38°20.563' N., Long. 76°30.020' W., then running 116° True to a point defined by Lat. 38°20.518' N., Long. 76°29.905' W., then running 194° True to a point defined by Lat. 38°20.410' N., Long. 76°29.940' W., then running 247° True to a point at or near Lat. 38°20.380' N., Long. 76°30.028' W., then running along the shore to the point of beginning.
Speed Limits.
The speed limit for the area described in §B(1)(a) of this regulation is 6 knots Saturdays, Sundays, and State holidays, all year.
The speed limit for the area described in §B(1)(b) of this regulation is 6 knots Saturdays, Sundays, and State holidays during the boating season.

Lewis Creek encompasses the area beginning at a point, at or near Lat. 38° 18.752’ N., Long. 76° 28.607’ W., then running 65° (True) to a point, at or near Lat. 38° 18.763’ N., Long. 76° 28.575’ W., and running to the head of the creek. This area has a 6-knot speed limit Saturdays, Sundays, and State holidays, all year.

Mill Creek—St. Mary’s County encompasses the area beginning at a point, at or near Lat. 38° 20.007’ N., Long. 76° 30.345’ W., then running 323° (True) to a point, at or near Lat. 38° 20.123’ N., Long. 76° 30.455’ W., and running to the head of the creek, including all tributaries. This area has a 6-knot speed limit Saturdays, Sundays, and State holidays, all year.

Town Creek encompasses the area beginning at a point, at or near Lat. 38° 18.987’ N., Long. 76° 28.675’ W., then running 212° (True) to a point, at or near Lat. 38° 18.845’ N., Long. 76° 28.788’ W., and running to the head of the creek, including all tributaries. This area has a 6-knot speed limit all year.

Chalk Point Canal encompasses the entire canal south of a line beginning at a point, at or near Lat. 38° 33.822’ N., Long. 76° 40.797’ W., then running 158° (True) to a point, at or near Lat. 38° 33.790’ N., Long. 76° 40.780’ W., and from there to the head of the canal. This area has a minimum wake zone all year.

Sam Abell Cove encompasses the area beginning at a point at the east side of the mouth of Sam Abell Cove, at or near Lat. 38° 19.955’ N., Long. 76° 30.228’ W., then running 299° (True) to a point, at or near Lat. 38° 20.007’ N., Long. 76° 30.345’ W., and running to the head of the cove. This area has a 6-knot speed limit on Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays, all year.

para.text: In this chapter, the documents set forth in §B of this regulation are adopted.

In compliance with Business Occupations and Professions Article, §§12-101(p) and 12-205(a), Annotated Code of Maryland, the Board adopts the most recent version of the following Codes as they may be amended, modified, or updated from time to time:
International Mechanical Code (International Code Council, 500 New Jersey Ave., N.W., 6th Floor, Washington, D.C. 20001), which has been incorporated by reference in COMAR 09.12.50.02-1B(5); and

International Residential Code for One- and Two-Family Dwellings (International Code Council, 500 New Jersey Ave., N.W., 6th Floor, Washington, D.C. 20001), which has been incorporated by reference in COMAR 09.12.50.02-1B(4) and 09.12.51.04A(2) except Chapters 1—11 and 25—44.

The requirements of this chapter do not apply to work areas subject to the Maryland Building Rehabilitation Code as adopted by the Department of Labor under Public Safety Article, §12-1004, Annotated Code of Maryland, except as provided by that Code.

para.text: In COMAR 09.34.06, COMAR 09.34.07, COMAR 09.34.08, and COMAR 09.34.09, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.

“Advertisement” means the publication, dissemination, or circulation of any oral or written matter, including labeling, which directly or indirectly calls to the attention of the public the goods or services one has to offer.
“Alkaline hydrolysis” means the process of reducing human remains using water, alkaline chemicals, and heat inside a watertight vessel to accelerate decomposition.
“Authorizing agent” means an individual who has legal authority to arrange for and make decisions regarding the final disposition of human remains, including cremation or alkaline hydrolysis.
“Board” means the Board of Morticians and Funeral Directors.
Consumer.
“Consumer” means an actual or prospective purchaser of consumer services, consumer goods, or consumer credit.

“Consumer” includes an individual directed by the consumer, or properly acting on behalf of a deceased consumer, to inquire about the cost or purchase of cremation services or cremation related goods, or to act on behalf of the consumer.
“Consumer” includes a funeral establishment contracting with a crematory or the authorizing agent of a decedent contracting with a crematory.
“Cremated human remains” means the recoverable bone fragments of a deceased person that result from the completion of cremation.
“Cremation” means disposition of a dead human body by means of incineration.
“Cremation container” means a readily combustible, rigid container in which human remains are sent to the crematory and then placed in the cremator for cremation.
“Cremator” means the machinery within which the process of cremation begins.
“Crematory” means a building, portion or a building, or structure that houses the necessary appliances and facilities for cremation, alkaline hydrolysis, or both.
“Decedent” means a dead human being.
“Embalming” means the disinfection or preserving of human remains by arterial or cavity injection or any other type of preservation.
“Engage in the operation of a crematory” means controlling or managing a crematory.
“Holding facility” means an area, within or adjacent to a crematory, designed for the retention of human remains before cremation or alkaline hydrolysis.
Human Remains.
“Human remains” means:
The body of a deceased person; or
A part of a body or limb that has been removed from a living person.
“Human remains” includes the body or part of a body or limb in any state of decomposition.

“Hydrolysis container” means a container, including a body bag, in which human remains are sent to the crematory for alkaline hydrolysis, but the human remains may be removed prior to the start of the process of alkaline hydrolysis.
“Hydrolysis unit” means the watertight vessel in which the process of alkaline hydrolysis occurs.
“Hydrolyzed human remains” means the recoverable bone fragments of a deceased person that result from the completion of alkaline hydrolysis.
“Office” means the Office of Cemetery Oversight.
“Permit” means a permit issued by the Office to allow a person to operate a business which engages in the operation of a crematory or to act as a registered crematory operator.

“Permit holder” means the holder of a permit under Business Regulation Article, Title 5, Annotated Code of Maryland, to engage in the operation of a crematory or to act as a registered crematory operator.
“Person” means an individual, receiver, trustee, corporation, limited liability company, or partnership.
“Processed human remains” means the end result of pulverization.

“Pulverization” means the process following cremation or alkaline hydrolysis of crushing, grinding, or milling cremated or hydrolyzed human remains into fine particles or powder, leaving bone fragments reduced to 5 millimeters or less.
“Registered crematory operator” means an individual registered to operate a crematory as a sole proprietor or on behalf of a sole proprietor or permit holder.

“Responsible party” means a sole proprietor or the individual designated by a corporation, limited liability company, or partnership, which holds a permit to engage in the operation of a crematory, to be responsible for the operations of the crematory.
“Sealable container” means any container in which processed human remains can be placed and sealed so as to prevent leakage of the processed human remains or the entry of any foreign material.

annotations.annotation: Business Regulation Article, §5-204; Health-General Article, §5-508; Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: March 31, 2014 (41:6 Md. R. 378)
Regulation .02B amended effective April 13, 2026 (53:7 Md. R. 327)

para.text: A person who holds a permit or registration under Business Regulation Article, Title 5, Annotated Code of Maryland, and owns a greater interest in a crematory than a licensee under Health Occupations Article, Title 7, Annotated Code of Maryland, shall obtain a permit to engage in the operation of a crematory from the Office.

A person whose ownership interest in a crematory is equal to the ownership interest in the crematory of a licensee under Health Occupations Article, Title 7, Annotated Code of Maryland, shall obtain a permit to engage in the operation of a crematory from the Office.

A person who owns a crematory and is neither a licensee under Health Occupations Article, Title 7, Annotated Code of Maryland, nor a registrant or permit holder under Business Occupations Article, Title 5, Annotated Code of Maryland, shall obtain a permit to engage in the operation of a crematory from the Office.

A person who is a licensee or holder of a corporation license under Health Occupations Article, Title 7, Annotated Code of Maryland, and owns a greater interest in a crematory than a person who holds a permit or registration under Business Regulation Article, Title 5, Annotated Code of Maryland, shall obtain a permit to engage in the operation of a crematory from the Board.

An individual, who is designated as the registered crematory operator by a person required to obtain a permit to operate a crematory from the Office, shall obtain a registered crematory operator permit from the Office.
All operating crematories and individuals designated as the registered crematory operator required to obtain a permit from the Office shall apply to the Office for a permit.
A permit issued by the Office shall specify if the crematory is authorized to offer and perform either cremation services, alkaline hydrolysis services, or both.

section.heading: Permit — Requirements for Permit for a Crematory Owned by a Corporation, Limited Liability Company, or Partnership.

para.text: A corporation, limited liability company, or partnership shall receive a permit to operate a crematory in this State if the corporation, limited liability company, or partnership meets the permit requirements of this regulation.
A corporation, limited liability company, or partnership shall receive a permit if the entity:
Completes an application form;
Pays the nonrefundable application fee and any other fees due under this chapter;
Provides the name, address, and phone number of each affiliated crematory;
Designates a registered crematory operator as the responsible party for each affiliated crematory provided that:
The registered crematory operator may not be designated as the responsible party for more than a total of two crematories; and
A registered crematory operator shall be registered to operate a cremator, an alkaline hydrolysis unit, or both;
Provides a list of the officers, directors, members, partners, agents, and employees of the entity applying for the permit;

Provides a certificate of status, issued by the Maryland Department of Assessments and Taxation, indicating that the entity is in good standing, or its equivalent as determined by the Office, and dated not earlier than 30 days before the date of the application; and
Provides an affidavit stating that:
No federal or State taxes or fees are delinquent; and
The corporation, limited liability company or partnership is financially stable.

section.heading: Permit — Requirements for Permit for a Crematory Owned by a Sole Proprietor.

para.text: A sole proprietor applicant for a crematory permit shall receive a permit to operate a crematory in this State if the sole proprietor applicant meets the permit requirements of this regulation.
A sole proprietor shall receive a permit if the sole proprietor:
Completes an application form;
Pays the nonrefundable application fee and any other fees due under this chapter;
Provides the name, address, and phone number of each affiliated crematory;
Designates a registered crematory operator as the responsible party for each affiliated crematory provided that:
The registered crematory operator may not be designated as the responsible party for more than a total of two crematories; and
A registered crematory operator shall be registered to operate a cremator, alkaline hydrolysis unit, or both; and
Provides an affidavit stating that:
No federal or State taxes or fees are delinquent; and
The crematory business is financially stable.

section.heading: Permit — Requirements for Registration for a Registered Crematory Operator.

para.text: An individual shall register with and receive a permit from the Office as a registered crematory operator if the individual meets the registration requirements of this regulation. The permit shall specify whether the individual’s training and registration required by this chapter permits the individual to operate a cremator, alkaline hydrolysis unit, or both.
An individual shall be registered with the Office as a registered crematory operator if the individual:
Completes an application form;
Pays the nonrefundable application fee and any other fees due under this chapter;
Is 18 years old or older;

Is of good character and reputation and provides at least three character references from employers, community leaders, or professional societies, that the applicant is an individual of good character and reputation;

Has achieved certification as a crematory operator by the Cremation Association of North America, International Cemetery, Cremation and Funeral Association, or other equivalent certification recognized by the Office and the Board;
Has successfully completed the operator training course of the manufacturer of the cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit located in the crematory with which the applicant is affiliated;
States the name and mailing address of the crematory with which the applicant is affiliated;
States whether the crematory with which the applicant is affiliated is owned or controlled by:
A corporation;
A partnership;
A limited liability company; or
A sole proprietorship; and
States whether the applicant is the responsible party for the affiliated crematory.
Additional Requirements.
An individual applying for a permit as a registered crematory operator shall provide the Office with a detailed description of activities including, but not limited to, whether the applicant has:
Had a license, certification, registration, or permit of the type for which the application is being made ever denied, suspended, or revoked by any jurisdiction;
Been convicted of a felony in any State or federal court;
Been convicted in any State or federal court of a misdemeanor directly related to the ownership or operation of a crematory;
Had any civil judgments or settlements within the 5 years before applying for a permit which directly related to the ownership or operation of a crematory; or

Been convicted by a court in this State of violating a usury provision under Commercial Law Article, Title 12, Annotated Code of Maryland, or an unfair and deceptive trade practices provision under Commercial Law Article, Title 13, Annotated Code of Maryland.
An applicant shall submit copies of all:

Charging documents which were issued and docket entries for convictions, which occurred before applying for a permit, for a misdemeanor or felony referred to in §C(1)(b),(c), or (e) of this regulation;

Disciplinary actions, judgments, and final orders which occurred or were issued before applying for a permit for any regulatory probation, suspension, or revocation referred to in §C(1)(a) of this regulation; and
Civil judgments or settlements against the applicant within the past 5 years which directly relate to the ownership or operation of a crematory.

An individual applying for a permit, who is designated by the permit holder as the responsible party, shall state the name and residential address of each employee who sells goods or services to the public while engaging in the operation of a crematory.
A registered crematory operator may not be designated as the responsible party for more than two crematories.
An individual may be issued permits for affiliations with more than one crematory if each affiliated crematory is owned by the same individual or entity.

section.heading: Permit — Requirements for Permit for a New Crematory Owned by a Corporation, Limited Liability Company, or Partnership.

para.text: A corporation, limited liability company, or partnership shall receive a permit to operate a new crematory in this State if the corporation, limited liability company, or partnership meets the permit requirements of this regulation.
A corporation, limited liability company, or partnership shall receive a permit from the Office if, at least 30 days prior to the opening of the new crematory, the entity:
Completes an application form;
Pays the nonrefundable application fee and any other fees due under this chapter;
Provides the name, address, and phone number of each affiliated crematory;
Designates a registered crematory operator as the responsible party for each affiliated crematory provided that:
The registered crematory operator may not be designated as the responsible party for more than a total of two crematories; and
A registered crematory operator shall be registered to operate a cremator, alkaline hydrolysis unit, or both.
Provides a list of the officers, members, partners, agents, and employees of the entity applying for the permit; and
Provides an affidavit stating that the corporation, limited liability company, or partnership:
Is financially stable; and
Has the ability to continue to operate the crematory business for a 2-year period after issuance of a permit.

section.heading: Permit — Requirements for a New Crematory Owned by a Sole Proprietor.

para.text: A sole proprietor applicant for a new crematory shall receive a permit to operate if the sole proprietor applicant meets the permit requirements of this regulation.
A sole proprietor shall receive a permit from the Office if, at least 30 days prior to the opening of the new crematory, the sole proprietor applicant:
Completes an application form;
Pays the nonrefundable application fee and any other fees due under this chapter;
Provides the name, address, and phone number of each affiliated crematory;
Designates a registered crematory operator as the responsible party for each affiliated crematory provided that:
The registered crematory operator may not be designated as the responsible party for more than a total of two crematories; and
A registered crematory operator shall be registered to operate a cremator, alkaline hydrolysis unit, or both; and
Provides an affidavit stating that the new crematory business:
Is financially stable; and
Has the ability to continue to operate the crematory business for a 2-year period after issuance of a permit.
Before applying to the Office for a permit, a crematory shall have in its employ an individual who holds a certification from:
The Cremation Association of North America;
The International Cemetery, Cremation and Funeral Association; or
Another equivalent body recognized by the Office and the Board.

section.heading: Cremation Prior to Obtaining a Permit.
section.text: A crematory may cremate or hydrolyze the human remains of one decedent prior to obtaining a permit from the Office, provided:

para.text: The cremation or alkaline hydrolysis is only performed as part of the manufacturer’s operator training course for the cremation machinery or alkaline hydrolysis unit in which the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis takes place; and
The person or persons with the right to final disposition of the decedent are notified and have given permission prior to the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis.

annotations.annotation: Business Regulation Article, §§5-102, 5-204, 5-205, 5-301—5-310, 5-312, 5-401—5-403, and 5-901—5-905, Annotated Code of Maryland

para.text: A crematory shall be available for inspection by a representative of the Office at any time during operating hours. The permit holder or registered crematory operator shall be available to accompany the inspector during the inspection and sign the initial inspection report. A copy of the inspection report shall be provided to the permit holder or registered crematory operator.
Each crematory shall be inspected:
On at least a biennial basis;
In furtherance of an investigation; or
Upon the sale or change of ownership of the crematory.
A crematory shall maintain the following minimum standards:
The premises shall be maintained in a sanitary manner to comply with Centers for Disease Control’s guidelines on universal precautions;

Except by express, written consent of the authorizing agents to perform simultaneous, multiple cremations or alkaline hydrolyses, there shall be no more than one human body cremated or hydrolyzed in a single cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit at a time;
A cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit shall be cleaned completely after each cremation or alkaline hydrolysis;
There may not be co-mingling of human remains and pet remains in refrigeration units, cremators, or alkaline hydrolysis units;
Separate cremators or alkaline hydrolysis units shall be dedicated for the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis of human remains and the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis of pets;
There may not be:
Co-mingling of processed human remains with other processed human remains or pet remains;
Scooping of processed human remains from a bucket containing commingled processed human remains; or
Any form of misrepresentation in the return of processed human remains;

Excluding a deceased person with a known communicable infection, human remains shall be properly identified before cremation or alkaline hydrolysis by verifying that the documentation accompanying the human remains is consistent with a visual observation of the human remains;
A burial transit permit shall be an original document pertaining to the deceased;
Each crematory shall have:
A sink with hot and cold water in the room in which the crematory equipment is housed; and
The tools necessary to thoroughly clean the floor within the room housing the crematory equipment with water and an appropriate sanitizing agent;
The name of the deceased shall be visible on the outside of the crematory container;
Human remains shall be properly stored prior to cremation or alkaline hydrolysis;
Crematories shall:
Use only mechanical pulverizing equipment meeting industry standards that has been thoroughly brushed as clean as possible between each use; and
Pulverize the processed human remains with a mortar and pestle when the processed human remains are not sufficient in amount for pulverizing in mechanical equipment;
Separate pulverizing drums shall be dedicated for processed human remains and processed pet remains;
Before pulverizing, the mechanical pulverizer shall be clamped;
A crematory may not refuse to release processed human remains pending payment of outstanding fees;
Cremators without safety devices that prevent the automatic door from dropping unintentionally shall be retrofitted with such safety devices;
Portable fans may not be used in the area of the cremator or pulverizing equipment;
There shall be a minimum of 6 inches between a cremator smoke stack and the roof of the crematory;
A cremator shall have, visible on its front, a warning sign stating “No leaning past the door of the cremator.”;
The permit holder or registered crematory operator shall be present at the crematory during regular business hours; and

All individuals who operate the cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit in a crematory shall be certified by the Cremation Association of North America, International Cemetery, Cremation and Funeral Association, or other equivalent certification recognized by the Office and the Board. Individuals receiving training toward certification to operate a cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit shall be allowed to work under the supervision of a registered crematory operator who has the required certification for a period not to exceed 6 months.
The following completed forms shall be available at all times for inspection and copying and are subject to be pulled at random by the inspector:
Crematory services authorization form;
Burial transit permit;
Delegation of authority form;
Receipt for human remains;
Record of cremation or alkaline hydrolysis;
Certificate of cremation or alkaline hydrolysis; and
Return of processed human remains certificate.
Inspection results shall be written on forms approved by the Office.
The representative of the Office performing the inspection shall, upon completion of the inspection:
Apprise the permit holder of the findings of the inspection; and
Provide the permit holder or a representative of the permit holder with a copy of the inspection report.
The permit holder or representative of the permit holder shall sign and verify receipt of the inspection report.
At the time of the Office’s inspection, the permit holder shall provide written documentation to the Office’s inspector that:
The crematory equipment has been inspected in accordance with manufacturer specifications;
In accordance with manufacturer specifications, it is not time for a manufacturer’s inspection; or
The permit holder has requested that the manufacturer conduct an inspection of the crematory equipment.
A complaint regarding a permit holder or a registered crematory operator under the jurisdiction of the Office shall be:
Filed by the complainant, on a form devised by the Office in compliance with the provisions of Business Regulation Article, §5-311(b), Annotated Code of Maryland; and
Processed in accordance with the provisions of Business Regulation Article, §5-311(c)—(h), Annotated Code of Maryland.
The Office may initiate a complaint or investigation.

annotations.annotation: Business Regulation Article, §§5-204, 5-310, and 5-311, Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: March 31, 2014 (41:6 Md. R. 378)
Regulation .02 amended effective April 13, 2026 (53:7 Md. R. 327)
Regulation .04A amended effective April 13, 2026 (53:7 Md. R. 327)

section.heading: Scope.
section.text: This chapter governs the procedures to be followed by a permit holder in performing a cremation or alkaline hydrolysis in this State.

section.heading: Removal, Identification, and Transportation of Human Remains.

para.text: Upon receipt of human remains, a permit holder, in the presence of the authorizing agent or representative of the funeral establishment engaging crematory services, shall:
Verify that the information on the wrist tag of the human remains is consistent with the documentation accompanying the human remains and consistent with the visual observation of the human remains;
Use a metal detector wand to inspect for the presence of any battery operated, implanted devices including pacemakers, defibrillators, or pain relief devices;
Refuse to accept human remains for cremation or alkaline hydrolysis if an inspection of the human remains indicates the presence of any battery operated, implanted device;

Remove and properly dispose of any hazardous object or any other materials that the permitted designee of the permit holder deems should be removed from the human remains or crematory container in order to prevent harm to the public health or damage to the cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit;
Remove any jewelry on the human remains or in the crematory container;
Return any removed jewelry to the authorizing agent or representative of the funeral establishment engaging [cremation] crematory services; and

Obtain a signed, itemized receipt from the authorizing agent or representative of the funeral establishment engaging crematory services for the removed jewelry and retain the receipt as a permanent record.
Foreign objects removed from the human remains:
Shall be treated as medical waste and disposed of accordingly; and
May not be donated until a sterilization process through a third party, recognized by the Office, to dispose properly of medical waste has been performed.
Materials identifying the human remains placed in the custody of a permit holder shall contain the following information:
Name;
Date of birth;
Date of death;
Name of funeral establishment or authorizing agent;
Gender; and
Name and relationship of authorizing agent to the decedent.
A permit holder shall not accept unidentified human remains for cremation or alkaline hydrolysis.
A cremation container:
Shall be a readily combustible, rigid container suitable for cremation;
Shall provide a completely enclosed covering for the human remains;
Shall be resistant to leakage or spillage;
Shall be of sufficient strength and rigidity for ease of handling;
Shall provide protection to the health and safety of crematory establishment personnel;
Shall comply with all local, State, and federal governmental emissions regulations; and
May not be composed of metal or polyethylene material.
A cremation container which appears to be heavily coated with varnish, lacquer, or any other highly combustible substance shall be:
Placed in a cold cremator within which no cremation has occurred in the prior 4 hours; or
Coated completely with water before being placed in the cremator.
An alkaline hydrolysis container:
Shall completely enclose the human remains;
Shall be resistant to leakage or spillage;
Shall provide protection to the health and safety of crematory establishment personnel and the public; and
May be removed prior to the start of the alkaline hydrolysis process as long as the human remains are covered to protect the dignity of the decedent.

Unless prevented by emergency circumstances, human remains that have been designated for cremation or alkaline hydrolysis shall be cremated or hydrolyzed by a permit holder within 48 hours after receipt.

A permit holder may not hold human remains for cremation or alkaline hydrolysis unless the human remains are contained within an individual, closed cremation or alkaline hydrolysis container as required under Regulation .03 of this chapter.
A permit holder may not accept a cremation or alkaline hydrolysis container from which there is any evidence of leakage of the body fluids from the human remains therein.

Whenever a permit holder is unable to cremate or hydrolyze human remains within 48 hours of taking custody thereof due to emergency circumstances, the permit holder shall maintain the human remains in a refrigerated holding facility, either on or off site, at 44° F or less, unless the remains have been embalmed.

section.heading: Identification of Human Remains Immediately Prior to Cremation or Alkaline Hydrolysis.

para.text: Immediately prior to being placed within the cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit, a designee of the permit holder shall:
Verify the identification of the human remains; and

Place in the cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit the hard metal or plastic identification disc of the human remains being processed where it shall remain in place until the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis process is complete.
The designee of the permit holder who is operating the cremator may not leave the immediate area of the cremator during the cremation process
The alkaline hydrolysis unit shall be monitored at all times during alkaline hydrolysis.
Except as otherwise provided in this regulation, a permit holder may not cremate or hydrolyze human remains until:
The body has been identified as required by Health Occupations Article, §7-411, Annotated Code of Maryland;
The crematory has received:
A crematory services authorization on a form approved by the Office and signed by an authorizing agent;
If applicable, a written delegation document or facsimile; and
Any other documentation required by federal, State, or local law; and
The permit holder has documented that at least 12 hours have elapsed from the time of death of the individual whose remains are to be processed.
The crematory services authorization form shall:
Be provided by the permit holder to the authorizing agent, and signed by the authorizing agent;
Contain the following information:
The identity of the decedent;
Date of death;
The name and address of the crematory where the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis will occur;
The name and address of the authorizing agent and the relationship between the authorizing agent and the deceased;
Authorization for the permit holder to cremate or hydrolyze the human remains;

Indicate whether the decedent is a veteran, as defined in State Government Article, §9-90,1 Annotated Code of Maryland, or an eligible dependent, as defined in Business Regulation Article, §5-803, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Authorization to verify that the following have been removed prior to cremation or alkaline hydrolysis:
Implanted pacemaker or defibrillator; or
Any other materials that the crematory authority deems should be removed to prevent harm to the public health or damage to equipment;
A representation by the authorizing agent that radiological implant treatment has not occurred to the human remains within 5 days before cremation or alkaline hydrolysis;
A representation that the authorizing agent is aware of no objection to the human remains being processed by any person who has a right to control the disposition of the human remains; and
The name and address of the person authorized to claim the processed remains from the permit holder or accept the processed remains via registered mail; and
Be signed by the authorizing agent.
If an authorizing agent is not available to execute the crematory services authorization form, the authorizing agent may delegate that authority to another individual:
In writing; or
If located outside the area, by transmitting to the crematory authority:

A signed, notarized statement, electronically or by facsimile device, that contains the name, address, and relationship of the sender to the deceased and the name and address of the individual to whom authority is delegated; and
Once the authorizing agent is located in the area, a notarized statement, through the United States Postal Service attesting to the delegation of authority.

Upon receipt of the written delegation document or a copy of the delegation statement transmitted electronically or by facsimile device, the crematory authority shall allow the named individual to serve as the authorizing agent. The documents shall be signed by the authorizing agent in the presence of a notary.

A person signing a crematory services authorization form is deemed to warrant the truthfulness of any facts set forth in the authorization form, including the identity of the deceased whose remains are sought to be processed and the authority of that person to order such a cremation or alkaline hydrolysis.

A permit holder shall notify and obtain the written consent of the authorizing agent before the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis of human remains at a location other than the location named on the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis authorization form.
A permit holder shall maintain a copy of every crematory services authorization form required under this regulation as permanent records.
Crematory services authorization records are subject to inspection and copying by the Office.

A permit holder shall provide to an individual who delivers human remains for cremation or alkaline hydrolysis a receipt signed by both the permit holder and the person who delivered the human remains, that includes the:
Name of the individual from whom the human remains were received and the name of the individual's employer, if any;
Name and address of the crematory authority;
Name and address of the deceased;
Gender of the deceased;
Date of death of deceased; and
Verification of authorized burial transit permit.
The permit holder shall maintain a record of each cremation or alkaline hydrolysis which shall include the:
Name of the deceased;
Date of birth of the deceased;
Date of death of the deceased;
Gender of the deceased;
Name and address of the authorizing agent;
Date, time, and location of cremation or alkaline hydrolysis; and
Name of the individual who performed the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis.

The permit holder shall provide a certificate of disposition of processed human remains to the authorizing agent or funeral establishment that arranged for the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis that contains the:
Name of the deceased;
Name of the authorizing agent;
Date, time, and location where cremation or alkaline hydrolysis occurred;
Name of the individual who performed the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis;
Name and address of the person who received the processed human remains from the permit holder; and
If ascertainable, the location, including the name of the cemetery and plot location if the remains are interred, the manner, and the date of the disposition of the processed human remains.
The permit holder shall maintain a copy of every record and receipt required by this regulation as permanent records.
All records required to be maintained by a permit holder by this chapter are subject to inspection and copying by the Office.
Except as provided in §B of this regulation, a permit holder may not:
Require that human remains be placed in a casket before cremation or that human remains be cremated in a casket;
Refuse to accept human remains for cremation because the remains are not in a casket; or

Refuse to accept human remains for cremation because the remains are in a suitable, combustible wooden casket, but may request the authorizing agent or funeral establishment engaging the services of the permit holder to remove the metal mattress holder in the casket.

Human remains delivered to a permit holder may not be removed from the cremation container and the cremation container shall be cremated with the human remains unless the authorizing agent for the deceased requests that the human remains be placed in the cremator without enclosure in a cremation container.

The cremation container shall be cremated with the human remains unless the authorizing agent for the deceased requests a more natural environment for the deceased. A permit holder is not required to accept this manner of disposition.
A permit holder may not require that human remains be subjected to embalming before cremation.
Upon completion of the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis, insofar as is possible:
All of the recoverable residue of the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis process shall be:
Removed from the cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit; and
Except for medical devices remaining after cremation or alkaline hydrolysis, pulverized; and
Placed in a sealable container; and
The identification disc required by Health Occupations Article, §7-411, Annotated Code of Maryland, shall be:
Removed from the cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit; and
Placed in the sealable container with the processed human remains.
Medical devices which remain after the completion of the cremation or alkaline hydrolysis process shall be treated as medical waste and disposed of accordingly.
After pulverization, all of the processed human remains, together with the identification disc, shall be placed in a sealable container.

If the sealable container opening is not of adequate dimensions to accommodate an identification disc, it shall be affixed to the exterior of the sealable container and a record of the cremation number shall be placed in the sealable container.

If all of the processed human remains and the identification tag will not fit within the dimensions of a sealable container, the remainder of the processed human remains shall be returned to the authorizing agent, or the agent's representative, in a separate, sealable container. Container seams shall be taped.

If the processed human remains and identification tag do not adequately fill the container's interior dimensions, the extra space may be filled with packing material that will not become intermingled with the processed human remains and then securely closed.
If a sealable container containing processed human remains is to be shipped, the sealable container shall:
Be placed within a separate sturdy box with all box seams securely taped closed; and
Have the name of the deceased person whose processed human remains are contained therein legibly written on the outside of the container.

If processed human remains have been in the possession of a permit holder, as originally authorized by the authorizing agent, without instructions for disposition, for a period of 10 days or more from the date of cremation or alkaline hydrolysis, the permit holder may send the processed human remains, using the current mailing standard approved by the U.S. Postal Service, to the authorizing agent.
Veteran and Eligible Dependent Decedents.

Unless the authorizing agent directs otherwise, if a permit holder in possession of processed remains of a decedent veteran or a decedent eligible dependent shall provide the decedent’s identifying information to a veterans service organization, as defined in Business Regulation Article, §5-803, Annotated Code of Maryland.
The veterans service organization will notify the permit holder whether the decedent is a veteran or eligible dependent eligible for burial in a veterans cemetery.

If the unclaimed processed remains are those of a veteran or an eligible dependent, the permit holder may transfer the processed remains to a veterans service organization for the purpose of disposition of the processed remains.

If a veterans service organization does not take possession of unclaimed processed remains that qualify for a plot in a State veterans cemetery under State Government Article, §9-906, Annotated Code of Maryland, within 10 days after the permit holder receives the notification required this regulation, permit holder shall:
Notify the Department of Veterans and Military Families of the status of the processed remains for the purpose of the appropriate disposition of the processed remains; and
Transfer the processed remains to the Department of Veterans and Military Families for the purpose of the appropriate disposition of the processed remains.
A crematory offering cremation shall maintain, at its own expense, the following tool inventory:
Safety placement tool measuring a minimum of 49 inches in length;
Wire brushes for cleaning cremators;
Brush with fine bristles for cleaning pulverizers;
Mortar and pestle;
Funnel;
High temperature protective gloves and heat resistant leather gloves;
Hand magnet and metal detector wand to detect and remove metal from cremated human remains;
Tweezers to remove nonmetal objects from cremated human remains; and
National Institute for Occupational Safety & Health certified N95 filtering facepiece respirators.
A crematory offering alkaline hydrolysis shall maintain, at its own expense, the following tool inventory:
Suitable tools for cleaning the alkaline hydrolysis unit;
Brush with fine bristles for cleaning pulverizers;
Mortar and pestle;
Funnel;
Hand magnet and metal detector wand to detect and remove metal from hydrolyzed human remains;
Tweezers to remove nonmetal objects from hydrolyzed human remains;
National Institute for Occupational Safety & Health certified respirators; and

An emergency shower and eyewash combination unit meeting the criteria established under the American National Standards Institute and the International Safety Equipment Association Z358.1-2014 (R2020) or its successor.
The crematory permit holder or registered crematory operator shall:
Submit to the Office the number of persons not affiliated with the operation of the crematory who can safely be within the same room and within 10 feet of the cremator door; and
Verify to the Office that the crematory permit holder has submitted the information required in §A(1) of this regulation to their liability insurance carrier.

Crematories that begin operation after July 1, 2014, shall construct a viewing room the area of the cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit if the permit holder intends to permit more than two visitors present in the area of the cremator or alkaline hydrolysis unit during a cremation.

para.text: A permit holder shall:
Act in a manner that respects and protects the dignity of a decedent and the decedent’s family;
Conduct business in a reasonable, usual, and customary manner;
Comply with the State public health laws as set forth in Health General Article, §§4-215 and 5-501 et seq., Annotated Code of Maryland;
Comply with Business Regulation Article, Title 5, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Comply with the Commercial Law Article, Titles 12 and 13, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Implement and follow through on all arrangements agreed upon between a consumer and the crematory;

Provide appropriate services for and respect the rights of individuals without regard to age, race, creed, national origin, gender, disability, marital status, political belief, religious affiliation, social or economic status, or social preferences;
Comply with all local, State, and federal laws for regarding the disposition of human remains;
Be sensitive and responsive to the bereavement needs of a decedent’s family;
Provide a general price list in effect at that time, in accordance with the requirements of the Federal Trade Commission’s funeral rule found at 16 CFR part 453; and
Provide accurate information from the general price list to any person who requests information, by telephone, regarding cremation related goods or services.
In advertising, a permit holder may not include statements:
That are misrepresentations of facts;
That are likely to mislead or deceive because, in context, the statement makes only a partial disclosure of relevant facts;
Relating to fees without reasonable disclosure of all relevant variables so that the statement would not be misunderstood or be deceptive to a consumer; or
Containing representations or implications that in reasonable probability can be expected to cause an ordinarily prudent person to misunderstand or be deceived.
A permit holder or registered crematory operator may not:
Use, or participate in the use of, any form of communication to consumers containing a false, fraudulent, misleading, deceptive, or unfair statement or claim; or

Operate a crematory or perform a cremation or alkaline hydrolysis while under the influence of alcohol, an illegal drug, or a controlled dangerous substance which has not been prescribed by a physician.

annotations.annotation: Business Regulation Article, §§5-204 and 5-310, Annotated Code of Maryland

Regulation .02C amended effective April 13, 2026 (53:7 Md. R. 327)

section.heading: Incorporation by Reference.
section.text: The following documents are incorporated by reference:

para.text: Guidelines for Design and Construction of Hospitals, The Facilities Guidelines Institute (2022 Edition); and
2025 Hospital Accreditation Standards, Elements of Performance, The Joint Commission, (December 15, 2024).

Notwithstanding any other provisions of this subtitle, each hospital and residential treatment center shall be open to inspections by the Department to investigate and resolve any complaint concerning patient care, safety, medical and nursing supervision, physical environment, sanitation, or dietary matters.
Complaints.
To resolve expeditiously a complaint that alleges the existence of any non-life-threatening deficiency, the Department shall refer the complaint directly to the hospital.

If appropriate, issues relating to the practice of medicine or the licensure or conduct of a health professional shall be referred to the hospital and may be referred to the appropriate licensure Board for resolution.

If the Department determines that the hospital has not satisfactorily addressed the referred complaint or if the complaint alleges the existence of a life-threatening deficiency, the Department shall conduct an independent investigation. When conducting its independent investigation, the Department shall use the standards adopted by the Secretary under this subtitle and:
For an accredited hospital, the hospital accreditation standards of the accreditation organization that accredits the hospital;
For an accredited special rehabilitation hospital, the service standards adopted by the Secretary in Regulation .10 of this chapter; or
For a nonaccredited hospital, the standards adopted by the Secretary in Regulation .09 of this chapter.
Acute General Hospitals and Special Hospitals. Nonaccredited acute general and special hospitals shall comply with The Joint Commission’s Hospital Accreditation Standards.

Waiver Authority. The Secretary may, for good cause, waive compliance with the incorporated Joint Commission standards. The hospital shall justify the need for the waiver in the manner prescribed by the Department.
A licensed acute general hospital or special hospital shall comply with the Emergency Management standards outlined in The Joint Commission’s Hospital Accreditation Standards.
A licensee shall prepare an executive summary of the hospital's evacuation procedures to provide to a patient, family member, or legal representative upon request. The summary shall:
Describe the hospital's Emergency Operation Plan (EOP), which is discussed throughout the Emergency Management standards cited in §A(1) of this regulation; and
Describe the role and responsibilities of a patient, family member, or legal representative in the event of an emergency situation.

A licensee shall have a tracking system to assist the hospital, family members, government representatives, and other authorized stakeholders, to locate and identify patients in the event of displacement due to an emergency or disaster.
The facility shall be in compliance with:
The Facilities Guidelines Institute's Guidelines for Design and Construction of Hospitals; and
All applicable State and local governing laws, regulations, standards, ordinances, and codes for fire safety, building, and zoning requirements.
Any waiver or other modification of these requirements shall be addressed by the appropriate State or local authority having jurisdiction in accordance with established procedures.

para.text: For a newborn, certification begins on the day the child is born to an eligible woman and ends on the last day of the month of the child's 1st birthday.

For an eligible child, certification begins on the first day of the month of application, or up to 3 months before the month of application, if medical expenses were incurred during the earlier months, and continues until the day the child is determined ineligible.

A child who, on the day the child becomes 19 years old, is receiving acute inpatient services under this chapter and who, but for attaining that age, would remain eligible for Medical Assistance benefits, shall continue to receive benefits until the end of the stay for which acute inpatient services are furnished.
Individuals may be certified for a period of continuous eligibility as described in COMAR 10.09.24.12.
