para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“ABAA” means the American Board of Animal Acupuncture.
“ACAHM” means the Accreditation Commission for Acupuncture and Herbal Medicine.

“Acupoint injection therapy” means a form of acupuncture that stimulates acupuncture points by subcutaneous, intramuscular, and intradermal injection with approved sterile substances, specifically manufactured for non-intravenous injection.
“Acupuncture” means a form of health care based on East Asian medical theories of energetic physiology that describe the interrelationship of the body organs and functions of a human or animal.
“Applicant” means an individual who has applied to the Board for a license to practice acupuncture in this State.

“Auricular detoxification” means an acupuncture technique that involves needling the external auricle of the human ear to assist individuals undergoing detoxification from addictive substances and restore health.
“Board” means the State Board of Acupuncture.
“CCAHM” means the Council of Colleges of Acupuncture and Herbal Medicine.
“License” means a license issued by the Board to practice acupuncture in this State.
“NADA” means the National Acupuncture Detoxification Association.
“NCCAOM” means the National Commission for the Certification of Acupuncture and Oriental Medicine.

“Practice acupuncture” means the use of East Asian medical therapies for the purpose of normalizing energetic physiological functions including pain control, and for the promotion, maintenance, and restoration of health including, but not limited to:
Stimulation of a human or animal body by the insertion of needles;
The application of moxibustion; and
Manual, mechanical, thermal, or electrical therapies only when performed in accordance with the principles of] East Asian medical theories.
“Pro bono work” means work provided within the scope of practice of the licensee:
Without financial reimbursement; or
Without direct financial gain.
“TOEFL” means the Test of English as a Foreign Language.
To qualify for a license, an applicant shall:
Submit a completed application in the format provided by the Board;
Provide at the applicant's expense, in English, all documentation required by §§B and C of this regulation or copies of original documents with English translations;
Pay the necessary fees established in COMAR 10.26.01.03;
Submit a current copy of a:
Government-issued photographic identification; or
Passport-size photograph; and
Meet with the Board to review the application if requested by the Board.
To prove that the applicant is 18 years old or older, the applicant shall provide a certified copy of an official government or institutional record, if requested by the Board.
The applicant shall provide documentary evidence of having met one of the following standards for education, training, or demonstrated experience:

Graduation with a master’s degree from an accredited educational institution with a practice relevant course of training that is equivalent to or exceeds at least 1,800 hours in acupuncture, including 300 clinical hours, that is:
Approved by the Maryland Higher Education Commission;
Accredited by the ACAHM; or
Found by the Board to be equivalent to a course approved or accredited by the authority listed under §C(1)(a) or (b) of this regulation; or
Achievement of a:
Diplomate in Acupuncture from the NCCAOM; or
Passing score on an examination that is determined by the Board to be equivalent to the examination given by the NCCAOM.
An applicant shall demonstrate proof of proficiency in the written and oral communication of the English language by providing documentary evidence that the applicant has:
Completed at least 30 classroom credits from:
An English-speaking undergraduate school;
An English-speaking professional school;
A community college; or
An undergraduate or graduate program at a college or university;
Achieved a passing score on the TOEFL within the 2 years preceding license application; or
Provided proof of passing scores on the NCCAOM written and practical examination components in English.
The Board shall determine whether an application is complete as received, with all required documentation enclosed.
Within 90 days of its receipt of a completed application and required information or documentation, the Board shall approve or deny the application.
The Board may close an incomplete application when the information needed to complete the application is not received within 120 days of the submission date.

Upon approval of an application, the Board shall issue a document authorizing the applicant to practice acupuncture in this State in accordance with Health Occupations Article, §1A-304, Annotated Code of Maryland, and the regulations in this chapter.
Except with approval of the Board, an applicant may not withdraw an application while under investigation or after having received a notice of denial of licensure.

section.heading: Term, Renewal, Reinstatement, and Inactive Status.

para.text: Unless renewed, the term of a license is valid through the expiration date.
Renewal.
At least 1 month before a license expires, the Board shall send to the licensee a renewal notice that states the:
Date on which the current license expires;
Date by which the renewal application is to be received by the Board for the renewal to be issued and mailed before the license expires; and
Amount of the renewal fee.
Before a license expires, the licensee may renew if the licensee:
Submits to the Board a completed renewal application in the format required by the Board along with documentation that the licensee meets the requirements for a license;
Pays the renewal fee specified in COMAR 10.26.01.03; and
If requested, submits to the Board documentation that the licensee has complied with the continuing education requirements established under COMAR 10.26.06.
A licensee shall keep the Board apprised of the licensee's current address and telephone number or shall pay a fee as specified in COMAR 10.26.01.03.
A licensee has a grace period of 30 days after the licensee's license expires to renew retroactively if the licensee:
Otherwise is entitled to have the license renewed; and
Pays to the Board the renewal fee and late fee set by the Board.
If the applicant’s license expires, an applicant may not practice acupuncture in the State until the renewal application is approved by the Board.

Reinstatement. Thirty-one days after the expiration date of a license, a licensee whose license has expired without seeking inactive status may have the license reinstated upon meeting all of the following:
Submission of a completed reinstatement application in the format required by the Board;
Payment of the reinstatement fee, and renewal fee, specified in COMAR 10.26.01.03; and
One of the following:
Thirty hours of continuing education within the 2 years that are most recent to the requested reinstatement date; or
Passage of the acupuncture exam given by the NCCAOM within 4 years from the date of reinstatement.
Inactive Status.
The Board shall place a licensee on inactive status for a maximum period of 6 years if the licensee submits to the Board within 30 days after the expiration of a license:
An application for inactive status in the format required by the Board; and
The inactive status application fee specified in COMAR 10.26.01.03.
To reactivate a license from inactive status, the applicant shall:
Satisfy the renewal requirements in §B(2)(a) and (b) of this regulation that exist at the time the individual changes from inactive to active status; and
Complete 30 hours of continuing education as specified under COMAR 10.26.06.
The licensee shall renew inactive status every 2 years, but not to exceed 6 years, in the format required by the Board and pay the fee as set forth in COMAR 10.26.01.03.
Continuing Education.
A licensee applying for renewal shall complete 30 hours of relevant continuing education during the 2-year period preceding the expiration of the license which include the following:

At least 20 hours in formally organized programs which are relevant to the practice of acupuncture and Oriental medicine and are approved by the Board or sponsored by, but not limited to, the following organizations and their member organizations:
American Academy of Medical Acupuncture;
American Association of Acupuncture and Oriental Medicine (AAAOM);
Council of Colleges of Acupuncture and Oriental Medicine (CCAOM);
Colleges and universities accredited by the Accreditation Commission for Acupuncture and Oriental Medicine (ACAOM);
National Commission for the Certification of Acupuncture and Oriental Medicine (NCCAOM);
Society for Acupuncture Research; or
The National Acupuncture Detoxification Association;

Not more than 10 hours of formally organized training programs in Western science and medical practices, medical ethics, medical research, or cardiopulmonary resuscitation which are relevant to the practice of acupuncture and are sponsored by, but not limited to, organizations listed in §F(1)(a) of this regulation and the following organizations:
World Health Organization (WHO);
National Institutes of Health (NIH);
American Medical Association (AMA);
American Nurses Association (ANA);
American Psychiatric Association (APA);
Red Cross;
Local colleges; or
Local hospitals;
Not more than 10 hours of training in approved programs which will assist a licensee to carry out the licensee's professional responsibilities, including, but not limited to:
Management courses for a school administrator;
Foreign language training for translators of relevant texts;
Educational methodology for teachers of acupuncture;
Not more than 10 hours teaching acupuncture and related Oriental medical therapies in an accredited school or in a program approved for acupuncture continuing education;
Not more than 7 hours of pro bono work as outlined in §F(3) of this regulation; or
Not more than 7 hours in published writing or articles in acupuncture and Oriental medicine that are:
Peer-reviewed research or peer-reviewed clinically relevant articles in acupuncture and Oriental medicine; and
Limited to 4 hours per article.
Pro Bono Work.
Pro bono work shall earn 1 credit hour for each 3 hours of pro bono activity up to a maximum of 7 credit hours per renewal cycle.
Pro bono work shall be for the provision of acupuncture services provided through an organization offering humanitarian services such as, but not limited to:
Domestic or international victims in an emergency situation or disaster area;
Low income or underserved areas or populations;
Special needs populations; or
United States military personnel.
Upon completion of the pro bono work, the licensee shall obtain from the facility written documentation of completion of pro bono hours including:
The name of the facility;
The address where the pro bono work was provided;
The type of work that was done;
The number of hours of actual work provided for which the licensee desires credit hours; and
A statement guaranteeing that the work provided no financial benefit to licensee.
Licensees shall maintain the documentation of their continuing education credits for 4 years from the date of their acupuncture renewal.
If requested by the Board, a licensee shall provide proof of continuing education attendance by providing documentation of:
Name of the program;
Date of the program;
Number of continuing education hours earned; and
Name of the sponsoring organization.
Change of Mailing Address, Legal Name, or Email Address.
A licensee shall give the Board written notice within 30 days of a change of:
Mailing address;
Legal name; or
Email address if the licensee has previously submitted an email address to the Board.
Failure to notify the Board of any change of mailing address, legal name, or email address within 30 days of the change may result in a fee as set forth under COMAR 10.26.01.03.
To obtain certification from the Board as qualified to practice acupuncture on animals, an applicant shall:
Possess a current active license to practice acupuncture in Maryland;

Successfully complete a Board-approved specialty training program in animal acupuncture from a school accredited by ABAA, ACAHM, or approved by the Maryland Higher Education Commission that consists of a minimum of 135 hours of theory and clinical training and includes at a minimum in each of the following:
90 hours in diagnosis of energy dynamics and treatment of animals,
15 hours in comparative functional anatomy and physiology of animals,
15 hours in animal handling, restraints, and emergencies, and
20 hours in introduction to animal diseases and zoonotics that require the immediate attention of a veterinary practitioner;
Complete and submit the application provided by the Board; and
Pay the fee as specified in COMAR 10.26.01.03.
To practice acupuncture on animals, the licensed acupuncturist:
Shall possess certification for animal acupuncture from the Board;
Shall cooperate and consult with a veterinarian licensed in the State by:
Beginning acupuncture treatment on an animal only if the animal has been seen by a veterinary practitioner within the previous 14 days,

Adhering to the terms and conditions of treatment decided by the veterinary practitioner, including the degree of communication and collaboration between the veterinary practitioner and the individual practicing acupuncture, and
Reporting to the veterinary practitioner at the end of the animal's acupuncture treatment or at monthly intervals at the discretion of the veterinary practitioner; and
May not practice acupuncture on an animal for which the licensed acupuncturist has not been appropriately trained in accordance with this regulation.
Certification Renewal, Reinstatement, and Reactivation.

The licensee may, as a part of the 30 continuing education unit renewal requirement, complete at least 5 continuing education units directly related to the certification of veterinary or animal related acupuncture.

The licensee may, as a part of the 30 continuing education unit reinstatement requirement, complete at least 5 continuing education units directly related to the certification of veterinary or animal related acupuncture.

The licensee may, as a part of the 30 continuing education unit reactivation requirement, complete at least 5 continuing education units directly related to the certification of veterinary or animal related acupuncture.
The Board shall maintain a registry of all:
Licensed acupuncturists certified by the Board as qualified to practice acupuncture on animals; and
Animal acupuncture training programs that the Board approves.
On or after the effective date of this regulation, unless the person is certified by the Board as qualified to practice acupuncture on animals, an individual may not:
Represent to the public that the individual is an animal acupuncturist; and
Practice acupuncture on animals.

Exception to Certification. This regulation does not apply to an individual who practices acupuncture on an animal under a research protocol which has been approved by an Animal Use and Care Committee as provided in 9 CFR Ch. 1, Part 2, Subpart C, §§2.30—2.38.
Violation of this regulation constitutes unprofessional conduct and may result in disciplinary action as set forth in Health Occupations Article, §4-315, Annotated Code of Maryland.

Qualification. To qualify as an auricular detoxification specialist, an applicant shall be a certified or licensed practitioner in the State as one of the following health care professionals who works directly with chemically dependent clients of a chemical dependency program in the counseling or treatment of those clients:
A certified professional counselor—alcohol and drug;
A certified associate counselor—alcohol and drug;
A supervised counselor—alcohol and drug;
A licensed clinical alcohol and drug counselor;
A licensed nurse;
A licensed psychologist who works directly with chemically dependent clients of a chemical dependency program in the counseling or treatment of those clients; or
A licensed certified social worker—clinical who works directly with the chemically dependent clients of a chemical dependency program in the counseling or treatment of those clients.
A practitioner that is a medical doctor shall obtain a medical acupuncture certification specific to physicians in accordance with COMAR 10.32.15.

Registration. The Board shall maintain a registry of all certified auricular detoxification specialists. To apply for certification with the Board as an auricular detoxification specialist, an applicant shall:

Provide documentation of successful completion of a Board and NADA approved training program that consists of a minimum 70-hour basic training of which 40 hours are clinical training which includes the following training and subject matter:
At least 5 treatments during a 10-minute period with good point location and techniques;
The uses and limitations of the treatment;
Communal treatment effect and maintaining an appropriate atmosphere;
Clean needle technique;
The limited scope of practice of auricular detoxification and other ethical principles;
The implications of the role of auricular acupuncture for addition in the larger addition treatment context;
Preparation of written chart notes regarding status, treatment given, and patient response;
Adjunctive nature of auricular acupuncture for addiction and the importance of collaboration and communication with the treatment team;
The psycho-spiritual nature of recovery and the ability to respect and support the client's individual beliefs and cognitive process;
Methods to modify treatment appropriately and to respond to adverse clinical situations should they occur;
Sensitivity, compassion, and respect for clients; and
Understanding of addictions from the perspective of basic concepts of East Asian medicine;
Complete an application provided by the Board;
Provide the name and address of the acupuncturist licensed by the State who shall supervise the applicant;
Provide verification of active licensure in good standing in the State from the appropriate Health Occupations Board;
Provide verification that an applicant works in a State-approved professional setting that specializes in the treatment of addiction; and
Pay the registration fee in COMAR 10.26.01.03.
Certification Renewal.
A certified practitioner shall renew their certification every 2 years.
As part of the renewal, the practitioner shall provide proof to the Board of:
Licensure in the approved health profession;
Employment working directly with chemically dependent clients of a chemical dependency program in the counseling or treatment of those clients;
Employment working in a State-approved setting, working with chemically dependent patients of a chemical dependency program in the counseling or treatment of those patients; and
Completion of three continuing education units relevant to the practice of auricular detoxification and the counseling or treatment of chemically dependent patients.
At least 1 month before a certification expires, the Board shall send a renewal notice to an auricular detoxification specialist that states:
The date the current certification expires;
The date the renewal application is to be received by the Board for the renewal to be issued before the certification expires; and
The amount of the renewal fee.
Before a certification expires, a practitioner certified to perform auricular detoxification may renew the certification if the individual submits:
A completed renewal application in the format required by the Board; and
Verification from the appropriate Health Occupation Board under §A of this regulation, that confirms that the individual has a current certification or license in good standing; and
The renewal fee specified in COMAR 10.26.01.03.
An individual registered to perform auricular detoxification shall maintain a current address and telephone number with the Board.
A practitioner listed on the registry to perform auricular detoxification:
May not practice using any other name than the name on their certification; and
Shall immediately inform the Board of any name change.
Certification Reinstatement. An auricular detoxification specialist holding an expired certification may apply for reinstatement if the individual:
Completes an application for reinstatement approved by the Board;
Submits the auricular detoxification specialist registration reinstatement fee specified in COMAR 10.26.01.03; and
Submits proof of completion of at least three continuing education units directly related to the certification.
Supervision. The Board may approve a Maryland licensed acupuncturist for supervision of a practitioner on the registry to perform auricular detoxification if the licensee:
Holds an active license in good standing to practice acupuncture in Maryland;
Has completed a training program in auricular detoxification as set forth in §C of this regulation; and
Submits to the Board a completed application in the format provided by the Board.
Supervisor Responsibilities. A supervisor shall ensure that:
Auricular detoxification specialists are sufficiently competent in the areas listed in §C(1) of this regulation;
The supervised auricular detoxification specialist and the Board are notified if the supervisor cannot or is unwilling to fill the role of supervisor; and
An on-site visit with the auricular detoxification specialist occurs at least once annually before renewal to:
View the setting where services are being provided; and
Observe at least five treatments, within an hour, performed according to the NADA group protocol.

Setting. The clinical substance abuse program or professional setting where an individual performs auricular detoxification on patients with documented substance abuse concerns shall meet best practices within community healthcare standards.
Complaints.
The Board may investigate a complaint filed against an auricular detoxification specialist or the supervising licensed acupuncturist.

The Board may withdraw the certification of the auricular detoxification specialist or take action against the license of the supervising acupuncturist if either individual violates an applicable law, regulation, or standard of practice while performing auricular detoxification.
Registration. To obtain the required registration from the Board as qualified to practice acupoint injection therapy, an applicant shall:
Possess an active license in good standing to practice acupuncture and East Asian medicine in the State;
Successfully complete an NCCAOM, or other appropriate accreditation body endorsed specialty training program and assessment in acupoint injection therapy that consists of at least 60 hours including:
A minimum of 40 hours of theoretical training in the required topics;
A minimum of 20 hours of live face-to-face clinical practical experience; and
Instruction that meets the requirements of §C of this regulation;
Possess a current certification by the American Heart Association or approved equivalent in basic life support;
Hold a current Clean Needle Technique Certificate through CCAHM or NCCAOM;

If licensed in the State for less than 2 years, possess a valid license in good standing to practice acupuncture and East Asian medicine, in any state, for at least 2 years before applying to perform acupoint injection therapy in the State; and
Complete and submit an application in the format provided by the Board.
The Board shall accept the documentation from a licensee that has completed the training required to perform acupoint injection therapy prior to the effective date of this regulation if:
The licensee provides a certificate, or other valid documentation, confirming successful completion of 60 hours of acupoint injection therapy training that complies with this regulation; or

The previously completed training does not meet the requirements of this regulation, but the licensee provides documentation of completion of supplemental training that, together with the initial training, fulfills the coursework requirements of §C of this regulation.
Training Program Curriculum. The curriculum for the acupoint injection therapy program shall include:
20 hours of clinical practice on a patient or patients;
History and development of acupuncture injection therapy;
Differential diagnosis;
Definitions, concepts, and pathophysiology;
The nature, function, channels entered, and contraindications of the substances stated in §F(2) of this regulation;
Diseases amenable to treatment with acupuncture injection therapy and the substances appropriate to treat them;
Identification of appropriate points for treatment, including palpatory diagnosis;
A review of physical examination, anatomy, and referral zones;
Universal precautions including management of blood borne pathogens and hazardous waste;
Procedures for injections, including preparing the substances, contraindications, and precautions;
Administration techniques and equipment required;

Administration of the prepackaged substances authorized for acupoint injection therapy, including aseptic technique, recordkeeping and storage of substances prepared and authorized for use in acupoint injection therapy;
Safety and emergency procedures; and
2 hours of training in the use of intramuscular epinephrine and emergency oxygen which count toward the required theoretical and clinical training hours.
Clinical Instructors. In addition to the requirements of NCCAOM, or other appropriate accreditation body, an instructor for acupoint injection therapy shall:
Ensure that the curriculum meets the training requirements set forth under §C of this regulation;
Be a licensed practitioner in acupuncture in good standing in the State and be registered to practice acupoint injection therapy in the State;
Possess at least 5 years of experience in the practice of acupuncture that includes acupoint injection therapy;
Retain proof of compliance as set forth under this section for as long as they provide acupoint injection therapy training; and
Provide proof of compliance to the Board upon request.
Training Programs. All training programs shall provide each successful student with:
A certificate of successful completion of the program;
A course syllabus outlining the schedule and curriculum of the program; and
Testing requirements and the performance measure for successful completion.
Practice.
To practice acupoint injection therapy, the licensed acupuncturist shall:
Possess a registration for acupoint injection therapy from the Board;
Explain acupoint injection therapy to the patient in advance of treatment;
Obtain written informed consent specific to acupoint injection therapy that shall be included in the patient’s medical record;
Only perform subcutaneous, intradermal, or intramuscular injection in accordance with the principles of acupuncture;
Have an adverse event or emergency plan in place for allergic reactions to injected substances;
Maintain professional liability coverage for this procedure; and
Only use prepackaged and sterile acupoint injectable therapeutic substances that are:
Acquired from an appropriately licensed entity in compliance with federal and State law; and
Stored securely and in accordance with manufacturer's specifications.

In the practice of acupoint injection therapy, the licensed acupuncturist, provided they have been trained on utilization of the specific therapeutic substance, may only use the following therapeutic substances for acupoint injection therapy:
Sterile and bacteriostatic water;
Saline solution;
Alkalizing agents such as sodium bicarbonate;
Nutritional or dietary substances;
Vitamins and minerals including B-12, B-complex, C, cyanocobalamin, hydroxocobalamin, and methylcobalamin;
Proteins, enzymes, and amino acids including methionine, choline, inositol, and glutathione;
Sugars including dextrose and glucose;
Ozone;
Procaine and lidocaine;
Herbal and botanical extracts including astragalus and Sarapin;
Biological products including hyaluronic acid and epinephrine; and
Homeopathic substances including Traumeel and Zeel.
A licensed acupuncturist in the practice of acupoint injection therapy may not:
Administer substances other than those specified under §F(2) of this regulation;
Administer substances that the licensed acupuncturist has not been appropriately trained in accordance with this regulation;
Give an injectable substance to a patient for self-injection; or
Administer intravenous injections.
The Board shall maintain a list of all:
Licensed acupuncturists registered by the Board as qualified to practice acupoint injection therapy; and
Acupoint injection therapy training programs endorsed by the NCCAOM, or other appropriate accreditation body updated at least annually.
On or after the effective date of this regulation, unless the person is registered by the Board as qualified to practice acupoint injection therapy, an individual may not:
Practice acupoint injection therapy; or
Represent to the public that the individual is an acupoint injection therapy registered acupuncturist.
Violation of this regulation constitutes unprofessional conduct and may result in disciplinary action as set forth in Health Occupations Article, §4-315, Annotated Code of Maryland.

section.heading: Name Under Which a Licensee May Practice.

para.text: A licensee shall be licensed to practice acupuncture only under the name indicated on the license.
A licensee wishing to change the name under which the licensee practices shall obtain prior Board approval.
The failure of a licensee to practice under the name on the license may:
Result in a fine; or
Be considered unprofessional conduct by the Board and constitute grounds for disciplinary action.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau” means the Tax and Trade Bureau of the United States Department of the Treasury.
Alcoholic Beverage.
“Alcoholic beverage” means a spirituous, vinous, malt, or fermented liquor, liquid, or compound that:
Contains at least one-half of 1 percent of alcohol by volume; and
Is suitable for beverage purposes.
“Alcoholic beverage” includes alcohol, brandy, whiskey, rum, gin, cordial, beer, and wine.

“Alcoholic beverage” does not include a confectionery food product that contains up to 5 percent of alcohol by volume and is regulated by the Maryland Department of Health under Health-General Article, §21-209, Annotated Code of Maryland.

“Board of license commissioners”, “local licensing board”, or “liquor control board” means a board of license commissioners or another governmental unit of a jurisdiction that issues alcoholic beverages licenses.
“Commission” means the Alcohol, Tobacco, and Cannabis Commission.
“Comptroller” means the Comptroller of Maryland or the Comptroller’s designee.
“County” means a county of the State of Maryland or Baltimore City.
“Executive Director” means the Executive Director of the Alcohol, Tobacco, and Cannabis Commission for the State of Maryland or the Executive Director’s designee.
“License” means an alcoholic beverages license issued or a permit granted under Alcoholic Beverages Article, Annotated Code of Maryland
“License holder” means a holder of an alcoholic beverages license, including:
A county liquor control board and a county dispensary; and

For the delivery and billing purposes of Alcoholic Beverages Article, §2-213 and Title 2, Subtitle 3, Annotated Code of Maryland, a corporation on behalf of which an individual has obtained a license.
“Sale” means whenever an alcoholic beverage is offered, given, paid for, or bartered for between one or more parties.

section.heading: Brand Size, Special Pack, Gift Packaging, and Labeling.

para.text: Definitions.
“Depletion allowance” means an allowance offered by a supplier which is:
For a given brand and size;
For a specific duration;
For a given amount per case;
Based upon sales from existing wholesaler inventories; and
Not conditioned upon purchases by a wholesaler.
“Private label” means any label that is owned by a licensed retail dealer, or is bottled and labeled exclusively for sale to a licensed retail dealer.
“Restricted or confined label” means any label of wine or distilled spirits, domestic or imported, other than private labels and those labels available for sale to licensed retail dealers generally.

“Self-imports” means any alcoholic beverages which are purchased and imported by a licensed wholesaler when the alcoholic beverages are invoiced by the foreign supplier direct to the Maryland licensed wholesaler.
“Special pack” or “gift pack” means a carton or package which:
Contains:
More than one brand of wine or distilled spirits, or
One brand of wine or distilled spirits and one nonalcoholic item; and
Is intended for ultimate sale to the consumer.

“Wholesaler” means a person who purchases from a nonresident dealer, or self-imports from a foreign source, any alcoholic beverages for sale to other licensed wholesalers or licensed retail dealers, and includes a county liquor control board and a county wholesale dispensary.
Special Pack or Gift Pack Requirements.
Size of Containers. Only one size of an alcoholic beverage container shall be represented within a special pack.
Containers in Carton. The total number of containers in a carton shall be in conformance with the provisions of §C of this regulation.

Nonalcoholic Items. Nonalcoholic items shall be restricted to those items which are physically packaged with the special pack and which are intended for ultimate purchase by the consumer. Signs, placards, devices, and graphic displays bearing advertising or other forms of advertising may not be included in special packs.
Consumer Oriented. A special pack shall be:
Entirely consumer oriented; and
Configured and packaged to show that it is intended for ultimate sale to the consumer.

Handling and Package Costs. The cost of special packaging materials and additional labor costs to assemble the special pack may be added to the price of the special pack if it is reported on a special pack price analysis.
Brand Identity. Each special pack shall be considered a distinct brand. Each special pack shall be assigned a unique UNIMERC brand code number as described in §B of this regulation.

Component Pricing. The cost of the special pack is not dependent on the price of any component alcoholic beverage also filed in regular case lots. However, special pack pricing may not be used to circumvent the prohibition on quantity discounts or be tantamount to providing free goods directly to the licensed retailer.
Approval Process.

Suppliers and wholesalers shall provide written communication to the Executive Director at least 14 days prior to its distribution, to include the type of alcoholic beverages, quantity amounts, size of containers, and any non-alcoholic items that are included in the special pack.

The Executive Director reserves the right to request more detailed information, including physical inspection or photograph, if a particular problem or question arises with regard to a specific filing.
Containers in Carton.
Packages are to be distributed as follows:
Multiples of 12;
Half quantities;
Four 5 liters;
Four 4 liters;
Four 3 liters;
Six 3 liters;
Six 1¾ liters;
Eight or 6 1½ liter containers, but not more than one configuration per brand in any 12 consecutive months;
15, 12, or 9 1-liter containers, but not more than one configuration per brand in any 12 consecutive months;
15 or 12 750-milliliter containers, but not more than one configuration per brand in any 12 consecutive months; or
Any legal size packaged and sold as a single container.

When containers hold less than 12.8 ounces or 378.7 milliliters, the quantity of containers may not be restricted to multiples of 12 but shall be in accordance with the prevailing practice in effect as to size and as to the number of containers in a carton.
Labeling.
A label that is sold to more than one licensed retail dealer in Maryland may not qualify as a private label, and shall be made available for sale to licensed retail dealers generally.
Restricted or confined labels are prohibited for sale to licensed retail dealers.
Terms or conditions of sale, including delivery charges, C.O.D. charges, etc., shall be stated on the invoice covering the sale.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau” means the Tax and Trade Bureau of the United States Department of the Treasury.
“Cigarette” means any size or shaped roll for smoking that is made of tobacco or tobacco mixed with another ingredient and wrapped in paper or in any other material except tobacco.
“Commission” means the Alcohol, Tobacco, and Cannabis Commission.
“Comptroller” means the Comptroller of Maryland or the Comptroller’s designee.
“County” means a county of the State of Maryland or Baltimore City.
“Executive Director” means the Executive Director of the Alcohol, Tobacco, and Cannabis Commission for the State of Maryland or the Executive Director’s designee.

“Retailer” means any person engaged in the business of making retail sales of cigarettes within the State at a store, stand, booth, or concession, through vending machines, or otherwise, and only applies to the retail sales of cigarettes portion of the person’s business.
“Sell” includes advertise, offer to sell, or offer for sale.
Wholesaler.
“Wholesaler” means a person who purchases cigarettes directly from a manufacturer and only applies to the wholesale sales of cigarettes portion of the person’s business.
“Wholesaler” includes a person, who, as a sub-wholesaler:
Purchases cigarettes from another wholesaler solely for the purpose of bona fide resale to retailers other than those directly or indirectly owned, affiliated, or controlled by the wholesaler; and

Services the retailers by maintaining an established place of business for the sale of cigarettes, including warehouse facilities, adequate inventory, proper accounting records, and necessary equipment and vehicles for the storage and distribution of cigarettes.

annotations.annotation: Business Regulation Article, §§16-205(a) and 16-209(b); Commercial Law Article, §§11-501(e)(1)(ii), 11-502(a)(5), and 11-507; Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date:
Transferred from COMAR 03.02.03 to COMAR 14.23.02 effective December 26, 2022 (49:26 Md. R. 1080)
Regulation .01B amended effective April 13, 2026 (53:7 Md. R. 328)

para.text: In this chapter, the following words have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Advertising sign” means a printed board, poster, placard, or similar material provided by a supplier or licensed wholesaler to promote a particular alcoholic beverage product or supplier.
“Alcoholic beverage list” means a list or other advertising piece which is provided to a specific licensed retailer or group of licensed retailers and includes:
Captain's books, table tents, and similar items; and
A list for wine, spirits, and beer.
Brand Identifiable Advertising Material.

“Brand identifiable advertising material” means a distinctive or generic name, trademark, logo, or symbol, identifying the product of a brewery, distillery, rectifier, bottler, manufacturer, vintner, or winery.
“Brand identifiable advertising material” includes proprietary or contracted logos and symbols.
“Brand identifiable advertising material” does not include logos, symbols, or other depictions that are not brand related.
“Brand owner” means a person or entity, including but not limited to a brewery, distillery, rectifier, bottler, manufacturer, vintner, or winery, who has:
Documented title for a specific brand of alcoholic beverages; and
If applicable, obtained label approval from the Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau.
“Bureau” means the Alcohol and Tobacco Tax Bureau of the Comptroller of the Treasury.
“Charity or nonprofit group” means an organization which qualifies for an exemption from federal income taxes under Internal Revenue laws and regulations.
“Commission” means the Alcohol, Tobacco, and Cannabis Commission.
“Comptroller” means the Comptroller of Maryland or the Comptroller’s designee.
“Consumer” means an individual of legal age who buys, possesses, keeps, or transports lawfully acquired alcoholic beverages for personal use only and not for sale.
“Contest” means a competition which is rated or scored by independent judges to determine a winner and when the winner is chosen by some skill, ability, or knowledge.
“Coupon” means:
A rebate offer by which a consumer, upon purchase of an alcoholic beverage product, is reimbursed monetarily by the supplier or independent clearinghouse representing the supplier; or
An offer by which a consumer, with or without consideration, may obtain merchandise, other than alcoholic beverage products, through a supplier or independent clearinghouse.
Executive Director” means the Executive Director of the Alcohol, Tobacco, and Cannabis Commission for the State of Maryland or the Executive Director’s designee.

“General advertising” means a printed, spoken, or visual public announcement paid for by a supplier or wholesaler to promote a particular alcoholic beverage product or supplier and does not identify or benefit a particular retailer or group of retailers.
Licensed Retailer.
“Licensed retailer” means the alcoholic beverage premises as described by the alcoholic beverage retail license as issued by the respective county or city license issuing authority.
For the purpose of an arena class alcoholic beverage license, each separate vendor or business entity handling alcoholic beverages is considered a separate licensed retailer.
“Nonresident dealer” means a person or entity that holds a nonresident dealer permit issued pursuant to Alcoholic Beverages Article, Annotated Code of Maryland.

“On-premises advertising” means point-of-sale material in the form of a sign, placard, display, or other item provided by a supplier or licensed wholesaler placed on a licensed retailer's premises and intended to promote a particular product or supplier of alcoholic beverages.
“On-premises promotion” means an activity:

Intended to promote a brand owner or brand owner's product which is sponsored by a supplier, licensed wholesaler or retailer, or a nonlicensee, including but not limited to a charity, radio station, or third-party promotional agency; and
Held on the premises of an alcoholic beverage licensed retailer.
Personalized Material.
“Personalized material” means any advertising sign, wording, or information which is unique to a retailer or group of retailers.
“Personalized material” includes but is not limited to:
The name of the retailer;
Product size, packaging, or pricing;
Menus; or
Information promoting a retailer's event or activity.

“Representative” means an individual who is employed by or acts as an agent of a brand owner, nonresident dealer permittee, supplier, or licensed wholesaler and includes the holder of a solicitor's permit.
“Retailer” means a person or entity that holds an alcoholic beverage retail license pursuant to Alcoholic Beverage Article, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Supplier.
“Supplier” means a person or entity that sells alcoholic beverages to a licensed wholesaler in Maryland.
“Supplier” includes a brand owner, nonresident dealer permittee, agent of the brand owner or nonresident dealer permittee, and the holder of a Maryland manufacturer's license.

“Sweepstakes” means a drawing where the winner is chosen by lottery, matching game pieces, or a game of chance, or similar type of event which does not require any skill, ability, or knowledge in order to be selected.

“Third party” means an independent advertising or promotional agency, or any other person who is contracted by a supplier or a licensed wholesaler to conduct promotional activities on the premises of an alcoholic beverage licensed retailer.
Wholesaler.
“Wholesaler” means a person or entity that holds an alcoholic beverage wholesale license pursuant to Alcoholic Beverages Article, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Wholesaler” includes a nonresident winery permit holder.
In General.

A neon or other sign, poster, placard, device, or graphic display bearing advertising matter, or any other form of advertising, for use in windows or elsewhere on the licensed premises, may be given by a supplier or licensed wholesaler to licensed retailer if:
The utilitarian value is secondary and only incidental to the value as an advertisement;

The total value of any single advertising item furnished for use in a licensed retail establishment at any time does not exceed $150 except, however, that a malt beverage sign manufactured by a beer wholesaler and provided to a retailer may not exceed $50; and
The cost of installation of an item does not exceed that which is usual and customary in that particular locality.

Generally, the total cost of advertising items for products produced by a given brand owner on the premises of a given licensed retailer may not exceed $600 by a brand owner of wine or distilled spirits.
Advertising or promotional materials, such as paper cups, matches, brochures, leaflets, circulars, napkins, placemats, calendars, or similar items, may be furnished to licensed retailers if the:
Advertising is general in nature;
Advertising does not identify a particular licensed retailer or licensed premises;
Promotional item is provided in trivial quantities; and
Promotional item does not relieve the licensed retailer of an ordinary business expense.

Advertising items, displays, and other promotional materials for nonalcoholic beverage products shall meet the same criteria and be subject to the same limitations as alcohol products if provided by a supplier or licensed wholesaler. To do otherwise could be construed as providing the licensed retailer with something of value contrary to applicable provisions of Alcoholic Beverages Article, Annotated Code of Maryland.

An alcoholic beverage advertising sign, display, or item on a licensed retailer's premises is subject to confiscation if it is contrary to the provisions of Alcoholic Beverages Article, Annotated Code of Maryland, or any regulation under this subtitle.
Advertising items may be furnished to licensed retailers in connection with a bona fide promotion as permitted by this regulation.
Distilled Spirits and Wine Advertising.
An advertisement may not be personalized for a particular licensed retailer or a licensee's retail promotion.
Advertising material for an activity common to all licensed retailers that is mass produced and contains prominent and permanent material is permissible if:
A licensed retailer is not identified; and
The utilitarian value is secondary and only incidental to the value as an advertisement.
Beer Advertising.

A manufacturer, malt beverage supplier, nonresident dealer permittee, or a licensed wholesaler may not provide newspaper, radio, or television advertisement to a licensed retailer directly or indirectly.

A sign, display, or other form of advertising shall contain brand identifiable advertising material that is prominent, permanent, and equal to the life and value of the utilitarian character of the advertising item.

An advertising sign, poster, or point-of-sale material may contain personalized material for a particular licensed retailer if the personalized material is not more than 50 percent of the total dimension of the sign, poster, placard, or other material.

An advertising sign, poster, or other point-of-sale material which contains personalized material shall contain brand identification which is equal to or greater than the personalized portion of the advertising sign, but in no case shall brand identification constitute less than 1/3 of the total area of the advertising sign.
If an advertising sign, poster, or other point-of-sale material has no personalized material, the limits and restrictions set forth in §C(3) and (4) of this regulation do not apply.
Retailer and Consumer Advertising Specialties.
Nonpersonalized items, such as trays, coasters, mats, paper napkins, cups, and similar items that advertise a supplier's brand, are permitted to be given to a licensed retailer in trivial quantities.
Specialty items may not be provided with a licensed retailer's name or logo placed on the item.
Retailer advertising specialties do not relieve the licensed retailer of an ordinary business expense.
Nonpersonalized items in excess of trivial quantities shall be sold to the licensed retailer at fair market value or higher if documentation of the sale is maintained.
Hats, towels, t-shirts, glassware, and similar items that have manufacturer or supplier advertising shall be sold to licensed retailers at fair market value.
Product Displays.
A product display includes wine racks, bins, barrels, casks, shelving, and similar items, the primary function of which is to hold, shelve, or display product.
A product display may not exceed the dollar limitations as described in Alcoholic Beverages Article, §§2-216 and 2-315, Annotated Code of Maryland.
A display may not contain merchandise contained in a consumer offer program.

A representative of licensed wine and liquor suppliers or wholesalers may not build displays on a licensed retailer's floor in excess of 15 cases. A display may be built anywhere on the licensed premises but may not be built onto a part of the existing shelving system at the retailer's establishment. A wine and liquor salesperson or merchandiser, or both, may build displays in excess of 15 cases but must make the appropriate charge back to the supplier. A beer salesperson may build displays and is not limited as to quantity if the advertising value of the display exceeds any secondary service provided to the licensed retailer.
A supplier and wholesaler representative may not use a licensed retailer's fixtures or equipment to build or maintain displays.

Except as provided in §E(10) of this regulation, when a display has been built, a salesperson or merchandiser may not rebuild the display, and a licensed retailer may not require a salesperson or merchandiser to move the display from one area of the licensed premises to another.

A salesperson or merchandiser may not use licensed retailer-supplied equipment, supplies, computers, etc. to make or prepare product or other signs at a licensed retail establishment. It is permissible for a supplier or wholesaler representative to place “bottle neckers” on their products if no licensed retailer-supplied equipment or materials are used.
An advertising item permitted by this regulation used in the display shall have brand identification that is conspicuous and permanent.
An advertising item with a utilitarian value shall be altered or disabled in a manner as to render it not usable for its normal purpose.

When a beer or malt beverage product display has been built, a salesperson, merchandiser, or driver may replenish or rebuild the display for purposes of product rotation at the time of a regularly scheduled delivery, merchandisers call, or sales call, subject to the provisions of Alcoholic Beverages Article, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Umbrellas.
A brand owner may furnish for display and use by a licensed retailer up to a total of 12 umbrellas advertising alcoholic beverages or nonalcoholic beer products, if:
The total cost of umbrellas does not exceed $600; and
No single brand is represented by more than six umbrellas.
Umbrellas permitted under this section shall be displayed on the licensed retailer's premises.
Umbrellas removed from the licensed retailer's premises may be returned to the brand owner.
Approvals of On-Premises Advertising.
It is not necessary to submit to the Executive Director for approval point-of-sale advertising items that are used consistent with this chapter.
The burden of proof that all on-premises advertising items meet the requirements of this chapter rests with the supplier or nonresident dealer permit holders.
A supplier shall submit for approval to the Executive Director advertising items of a questionable nature to assure they are operating within the provisions of this chapter.
Approval of Advertising and Promotional Contracts.

Before an alcoholic beverages supplier or malt beverage wholesaler enters into a contract for advertising and promotional services that will occur on the premises of a retail licensee in exchange for a sponsorship fee or other consideration, a copy of the contract, with supporting documentation, shall be submitted to the Executive Director for review.
A supplier or wholesaler shall receive approval from the Executive Director prior to entering into a contract under §A(1) of this regulation.
Description.
An on-premises promotion is an activity to promote a brand owner's product that includes:
Brand owner promotions;
Charity promotions;
Radio station promotions; or
Licensed retailer promotions.
An on-premises promotion includes tastings:
That are open to the public and held in conjunction with a reception or dinner; and
At which a representative of a supplier or licensed wholesaler:
Is present;
Discusses the characteristics and merits of a particular brand or type of alcoholic beverage;
Interacts with the attendees; and
May pour the representative's own products.
Brand Owner Promotional Activities.

A brand owner or supplier, third party, or wholesaler acting as a brand owner's agent may sponsor, organize, or participate in up to 24 promotional activities in a calendar year on behalf of the same licensed retailer. The limit is per brand owner or supplier and not per brand.

An event conducted by or on behalf of a licensed off-premises retailer at a licensed on-premises retail account may not be construed as one of the licensed on-premises retailer's promotions if the licensed on-premises retailer's sole involvement is to cater the event and provide the alcoholic beverages. In these instances, it shall be considered one of the events allotted to the licensed off-premises retailer.
A brand owner may provide brand identifiable prizes or give-away items to be used at approved on-premises promotions if:
The total number of cumulative items or prizes for any given promotion does not exceed 50 percent of the maximum capacity of the licensed retailer's premises as determined by the Fire Marshal;
The unit cost of any one item or prize does not exceed $10;
A brand owner or agent does not expend more than $150 in incidental expenses for any given promotion;
All prizes and other incidentals not used in connection with an approved promotional event are removed from the retail licensed premises at the conclusion of the event; and
Documentation on the expenses is maintained for each event and is available for inspection.

A brand owner, supplier, or licensed wholesaler may not provide alcoholic beverage products at no cost to a licensed retailer. All products shall be provided and paid for through the normal distribution channels.

A representative of a brand owner, supplier, or licensed wholesaler may attend and participate in an approved on-premises promotional activity and may spend up to $200 for the purchase of alcoholic beverage products at a licensed retail establishment at the regular price charged by the licensed retailer, including a reasonable and customary tip directed to service staff who are not owners, officers, partners, or named as license holders of the retailer.

In addition to pre-planned and pre-announced promotions that are permitted, a supplier or licensed wholesaler may make short, spontaneous, and unannounced visits to a licensed retailer's premises for the purpose of promoting a brand owner's product. Brand identifiable items or prizes provided during these visits shall be of nominal value and distributed in trivial quantities. See Regulation .06B(4) of this chapter.
Charity Promotions.

A charity or nonprofit group which holds a temporary alcoholic beverage license is considered a licensed retailer and is generally subject to all the restrictions and responsibilities of any other licensed retailer regarding trade practices.
A charity group which does not hold a retail license may receive legitimate donations from suppliers or licensed wholesalers when engaging in legitimate fund raising or other activities.
Holders of a charity wine auction permit may receive alcohol from other sources as provided in Alcoholic Beverages Article, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Radio Station Promotions.
Radio stations are considered third parties and are subject to Regulation .07 of this chapter.

When acting as a third-party agent for a supplier or licensed wholesaler, radio stations may not engage in any promotion on a licensed retailer's premises on behalf of a supplier or licensed wholesaler unless the supplier or licensed wholesaler is authorized by the Executive Director to conduct the promotion.
Retail Promotions.
A licensed retailer may sponsor promotional activities on the retailer's own premises.

All expenses related to the promotion which include, but are not limited to, prizes, entertainment, support materials, staff, media advertising, and alcoholic beverages, shall be paid by the licensed retailer.
Except as otherwise permitted in this chapter, a supplier or licensed wholesaler may not participate at a licensed retailer-sponsored promotion.
Supplier and licensed wholesaler personnel may be on the premises of a licensed retailer-sponsored promotion for observation purposes only.
A licensed retailer may not participate in the cost of advertising or promoting a wine or distilled spirits product.
A licensed retailer may use advertising items including “bar kits” which are purchased from a brand owner or licensed wholesaler as authorized by Regulation .10 of this chapter.
A supplier or licensed wholesaler may rent to a licensed retailer promotional materials approved by the Executive Director to be used at a licensed retailer-sponsored event if:
The daily cost of the rental is at least 5 percent of the original value of the materials;
A minimum daily charge is $20; and
Both parties to the rental agreement maintain documentation which is available for review by the Executive Director.
Approvals.
A person or entity who engages in on-premises promotional activities as authorized by §B of this regulation shall file a report on forms provided by the Executive Director.

A wholesaler or third party may file a promotional activity form on behalf of a brand owner only if they have written authorization to act as a representative of the brand owner and the authorization is provided to the Executive Director.

The report filed with the Executive Director shall list all proposed events and shall be filed not later than the 20th day of the month preceding the month in which the proposed event will be conducted.
Regular monthly reports are not acknowledged by the Executive Director.

A brand owner or supplier, as well as any wholesaler or third party acting as an agent of the brand owner, is responsible for complying with all provisions of this chapter including limitations on the number of events conducted.
Amended Report. An amended report:
May be accepted up to 5 days before the planned promotional activity if unusual circumstances warrant; and
Shall be approved by the Executive Director.
If an accurate and timely report is not submitted:
Each promotion shall be submitted to the Executive Director for approval; and
An acknowledgment must be received from the Executive Director before conducting the event.

para.text: Fees shall be assessed according to the requirements in §§B—I of this regulation.
Persons Issued a Nursery Inspection Certificate or a Plant Dealer License.
For field inspections of:
1 acre or less— $20;
More than 1 acre to 5 acres—$30;
More than 5 acres to 10 acres—$40;
More than 10 acres—$5 for each acre or part of any acre, up to a maximum of $1,500.
Nursery Inspection Certificate—$150 a year for each sales location.
Plant Dealer License—$150 a year for each sales location.
Persons issued a Plant Broker License. Plant Broker License—$150 a year.
Certified Seed Potatoes.
Production.
Field Inspection—$4 an acre or part thereof a year.
Bin Inspection—2 cents for each 100 pounds.
Imported Seed for Planting. Boxcar or Warehouse Inspection—8 cents for each 100 pounds.
Fees are due upon receipt of an invoice from the Department.
Trueness to Name Plant Certification.
Fees—$50 per acre or part of an acre in plant production.
Fees are due upon receipt of an invoice from the Department.
Virus-Free Plant Certification.
Certification Fee. Each plant producer shall pay the Secretary the following certification fee for each acre, or part of an acre, in plant production:
Strawberry plants—$50;
Grapevines, fruit trees, and bramble plants—$70.
Fees are due upon receipt of an invoice from the Department.
Miscellaneous Service Fees.
Soil sample analysis:
Fertility—$10;
Pathogen—$25;
Nematode—$25.
Pest identification:
Routine—$5;
Nonroutine—$15;
Urgent—$25.
Equipment fumigation:
Equipment pick up—$25;
Equipment return—$25;
Hive body/deep super—$4;
Shallow super—$3.50;
Miscellaneous equipment—$1.50.
Phytosanitary certificates:
Including travel—$25;
Excluding travel—$5.
Other Miscellaneous Fees—up to $25 for each:
Publication;
Inspection;
Analysis; or
Sample.
A federal, State, or local public agency is exempt from the license and inspection fees required by this regulation.

para.text: The Retirement Agency may not deny an individual who is a member of the Teachers' Retirement System or the Employees' Retirement System before December 1, 1973, the right to:
Continue as a member of the Teachers' Retirement System or the Employees' Retirement System; or
Receive the accrued benefits to which the member was entitled.
Employees Enrolled in the Teachers' Retirement System in Error.

An individual enrolled in the Teachers' Retirement System may continue as a member of the Teachers' Retirement System if the individual was enrolled before October 1, 1972, as a result of an administrative error.
A recommendation of the Executive Director to remove an employee, enrolled for a period of 6 months or more after October 1, 1972, shall be submitted to the Board of Trustees for approval.

section.heading: Membership in the Employees' Pension System.

para.text: Definitions.
In this regulation, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Contractual employee” means an individual who provides personal services to a participating employer for pay if all of the following conditions exist:
The services and pay are specified in a current written personal services agreement between the employer and an individual;
There is an employer-employee relationship between the participating employer and the individual; and

The individual does not receive any fringe benefits, except as provided by law or by statutorily authorized written policies adopted on or before November 6, 2017, which the employer has applied uniformly and which define a class of contractual employees who are entitled to receive specified fringe benefits.
“Emergency employee” means an individual who:
Is appointed by a participating employer to provide personal services on an emergency basis;
Is employed for a nonrenewable period not to exceed 6 months; and
Except as provided by law, does not receive any fringe benefits.
“Temporary extra employee” means an individual who:
Provides personal services to a participating employer;
Is employed for a nonrenewable period not to exceed 6 months; and
Except as provided by law, does not receive any fringe benefits.
The following individuals shall be excluded from membership in the Employees' Pension System:
Subject to §C of this regulation, a contractual, emergency, or temporary extra employee of a participating employer;
Employees of the Maryland Center for Public Broadcasting under the 9,000 series;
Resident doctors; and
School crossing guards who:
Are employed by a participating governmental unit:
On a substitute or relief basis, or
For less than 500 hours; and
Except as provided by law, do not receive any fringe benefits from the participating governmental unit.

The fact that a participating employer permitted an individual to enroll in the Employees’ Pension System shall not be relevant to the determination of whether the individual is a contractual, emergency, or temporary extra employee who is excluded from membership.

annotations.annotation: State Personnel and Pensions Article, §§21-108, 21-110, 22-207, 23-205, and 23-209, Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: January 1, 1996 (22:26 Md. R. 2031)

Effective January 1, 1996, all of Title 22 was repealed and completely new regulations adopted. Codification and organization of the title were also completely changed. Consequently, the administrative histories of the revised Title 22 do not reflect regulatory activity before January 1, 1996. Refer to the old version of Title 22 or contact the Maryland State Archives for assistance.
